conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me ask you some questions to assess your eligibility.

First, do you have any children you're asking about Child Benefit for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 18 years old and lives at home with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have a son named Alex who is 18 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is over 16, the key factor for Child Benefit eligibility is whether he's in approved education or training. Child Benefit can continue for young people under 20 if they stay in approved education or training.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs, or similar qualifications)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any approved education or training at the moment. He finished school and isn't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex is 18 and not currently in approved education or training, I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Has Alex started any paid work? If so, is he working 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started any paid work. He's not working at all at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One more question to complete the assessment:

Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not receiving any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

Based on the official UK government guidance, I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Child Benefit normally covers children under 16. For young people aged 16-19, you can continue to receive Child Benefit, but only if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 18 and is not currently in approved education or training, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for him.

**However, please note:** If Alex decides to return to approved education or training in the future (such as college courses, A-levels, or vocational training), you may be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point, as long as he remains under 20 years old.

If you have any questions about what counts as approved education or training, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for more information.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old, which means he is over 16. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you can still qualify if the child is under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in any approved education or training. He is also not working 24 hours or more per week (which would definitively disqualify him), and he is not receiving benefits in his own right (which would also disqualify him). The critical issue is that he does not meet the requirement for 16-19 year olds to be in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old and lives at home. While Alex is under 20 and living with the claimant, he is not in approved education or training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for young people aged 16-19. As a result, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
